description: M_TRANSFER proof-of-concept: Flask (Python) → Express.js (JavaScript) structural migration risk map. Pre-registered predictions for (A) Express law confirmation and (B) cross-technology migration risk classification via irdme atlas --compare flask.json express.json --pivot co_change. Flask is the confirmed M_EXT dataset. Express is extracted fresh via irdme extract with --scope javascript. The Universal Layer Grammar predicts both frameworks have the same three coupling levels: static_coupling (d1), structural_coupling (d2), co_change (d3). The law (r(d1↔d2) > r(d1↔d3)) should hold in Express because its architecture is as intentional as Flask's.
data_sources: github.com/expressjs/express — extracted fresh via irdme extract --scope javascript

hypotheses.statement: The law holds for Express: r(static_coupling, structural_coupling) > r(static_coupling, co_change). Express is a well-architected framework; its import graph and shared-require coupling should be more correlated than its git co-change behavior.

application is the top hub in Express static_coupling layer. application.js requires all other Express modules (request, response, router, view, utils) — it is the core app factory and should have the highest static import degree.

router_index or router is a top-3 hub in the Express co_change layer. The router is the behavioral core of any Express application — changes to request handling, middleware, and routing always touch the router module. It should be a top behavioral hub regardless of its static import rank.

At least 2 Express modules have cross-layer divergence (different rank tiers between static_coupling and co_change). The router subsystem is structurally peripheral (only application imports router directly) but behaviorally central (every route change touches it). This divergence — a hub shadow — predicts migration risk.

Flask app and Express application are the top structural match pair in the M_TRANSFER output (sim ≥ 0.65). Both are the declared entry points, top importers, and universal hubs of their respective frameworks. The co_change pivot should align them as the strongest cross-technology structural match.
